answer:
Tailwind Design Tokens — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

Definition

Tailwind Design Tokens are the foundational values (colors, spacing, typography, shadows, border radii) defined in tailwind.config.js that drive utility class generation. Design tokens create a single source of truth for visual consistency across an application. The deep dive covers trade-offs between Tailwind's utility-first approach and traditional design token systems (Style Dictionary), failure modes when tokens are misconfigured, and rollback strategies for token changes that break visual consistency.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

In ContactsHub, Manoj used Tailwind CSS with shadcn/ui for the React 18 frontend. He defined a design token system in tailwind.config.ts with semantic color tokens (primary, secondary, destructive, muted) mapped to CSS custom properties. This enabled dark mode support by simply swapping CSS variable values in the .dark class. When the design was refreshed (3rd generation: Vanilla JS → Angular → React), having tokens meant changing 12 CSS variable values updated the entire UI — no component-level changes needed.

Design Token Architecture

Code Example — Tailwind Token System

// tailwind.config.ts — design token definition
import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss';

const config: Config = {
  darkMode: 'class',
  content: ['./src/**/*.{ts,tsx}'],
  theme: {
    extend: {
      colors: {
        // Semantic tokens mapped to CSS custom properties
        background: 'hsl(var(--background))',
        foreground: 'hsl(var(--foreground))',
        primary: {
          DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--primary))',
          foreground: 'hsl(var(--primary-foreground))',
        },
        secondary: {
          DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--secondary))',
          foreground: 'hsl(var(--secondary-foreground))',
        },
        destructive: {
          DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--destructive))',
          foreground: 'hsl(var(--destructive-foreground))',
        },
        muted: {
          DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--muted))',
          foreground: 'hsl(var(--muted-foreground))',
        },
        card: {
          DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--card))',
          foreground: 'hsl(var(--card-foreground))',
        },
      },
      borderRadius: {
        lg: 'var(--radius)',
        md: 'calc(var(--radius) - 2px)',
        sm: 'calc(var(--radius) - 4px)',
      },
    },
  },
};

/* globals.css — token values */
@layer base {
  :root {
    --background: 0 0% 100%;
    --foreground: 222.2 84% 4.9%;
    --primary: 222.2 47.4% 11.2%;
    --primary-foreground: 210 40% 98%;
    --secondary: 210 40% 96.1%;
    --secondary-foreground: 222.2 47.4% 11.2%;
    --destructive: 0 84.2% 60.2%;
    --destructive-foreground: 210 40% 98%;
    --muted: 210 40% 96.1%;
    --muted-foreground: 215.4 16.3% 46.9%;
    --radius: 0.5rem;
  }
  .dark {
    --background: 222.2 84% 4.9%;
    --foreground: 210 40% 98%;
    --primary: 210 40% 98%;
    --primary-foreground: 222.2 47.4% 11.2%;
    /* ... dark values for all tokens */
  }
}

Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

After adding dark mode to ContactsHub, several shadcn/ui components appeared invisible in dark mode. The Card component used bg-card which mapped to hsl(var(--card)), but the --card CSS variable wasn't defined in the .dark class — it inherited the light mode value (white background on dark background = invisible card border). Fix: Created a token completeness check script that compared all :root variables against .dark variables and flagged any missing definitions. Also added a visual regression test in Playwright that captured screenshots in both light and dark modes for every page, ensuring no elements became invisible. The Tailwind JIT compiler also missed a dynamically constructed class bg-${color}-500 — fixed by using a safelist or static class references.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Dark mode variables missing → invisible elementsToken completeness script; visual regression tests in both themes
Dynamic class names not detected by JITUse safelist for dynamic classes; prefer static class composition
Token rename breaks all usages silentlyGrep CI check for old token names; TypeScript utility types for token names
Color contrast fails WCAG after token changeAutomated axe-core contrast check in CI for both themes
Tailwind tokens not shareable with Figma/nativeUse Style Dictionary as canonical source; generate tailwind.config from tokens

Validation Strategy

  • Token Completeness: CI script verifies all :root vars have .dark equivalents
  • Visual Regression: Playwright screenshots in light + dark mode per component
  • Accessibility: axe-core contrast check on every theme; WCAG AA minimum
  • Rollback: CSS var changes are instant (no rebuild); revert globals.css values
  • Design Review: Token changes require designer approval before merge

Tailwind Design Tokens — Scalable Strategy for 1M+ Users

Definition

Tailwind Design Tokens at Scale manages a design token system across multiple applications, teams, and platforms serving millions of users. The challenge is maintaining visual consistency across web apps (React, Angular), documentation sites, and potentially native apps while keeping the token system performant (CSS bundle size, runtime theme switching overhead) and governable (preventing token sprawl, enforcing brand guidelines).

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

The HR Leave Management System shared design tokens between the Angular 18 frontend and internal admin tools. Manoj created a shared token package (@hr-platform/tokens) that exported: (1) CSS custom property definitions for web apps. (2) Tailwind preset configuration. (3) JSON token format for Style Dictionary compatibility. When the brand colors were updated company-wide, updating the token package and bumping the version cascaded the change to all consuming apps via a single Renovate PR per repo. The Tailwind preset ensured consistent utility classes across Angular and React apps.

Token Distribution Architecture

Code Example — Shared Token Package

// packages/tokens/src/tailwind-preset.ts
import type { Config } from 'tailwindcss';

export const hrPlatformPreset: Partial<Config> = {
  darkMode: 'class',
  theme: {
    extend: {
      colors: {
        background: 'hsl(var(--background))',
        foreground: 'hsl(var(--foreground))',
        primary: { DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--primary))', foreground: 'hsl(var(--primary-foreground))' },
        secondary: { DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--secondary))', foreground: 'hsl(var(--secondary-foreground))' },
        destructive: { DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--destructive))', foreground: 'hsl(var(--destructive-foreground))' },
        muted: { DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--muted))', foreground: 'hsl(var(--muted-foreground))' },
        accent: { DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--accent))', foreground: 'hsl(var(--accent-foreground))' },
        card: { DEFAULT: 'hsl(var(--card))', foreground: 'hsl(var(--card-foreground))' },
        border: 'hsl(var(--border))',
        input: 'hsl(var(--input))',
        ring: 'hsl(var(--ring))',
      },
      borderRadius: {
        lg: 'var(--radius)',
        md: 'calc(var(--radius) - 2px)',
        sm: 'calc(var(--radius) - 4px)',
      },
      fontFamily: {
        sans: ['Inter', 'system-ui', 'sans-serif'],
        mono: ['JetBrains Mono', 'monospace'],
      },
    },
  },
};

// packages/tokens/src/css-vars.css
@layer base {
  :root {
    --background: 0 0% 100%;
    --foreground: 222.2 84% 4.9%;
    --primary: 221 83% 53%;
    --primary-foreground: 210 40% 98%;
    --radius: 0.5rem;
    /* ...all token values */
  }
  .dark {
    --background: 222.2 84% 4.9%;
    --foreground: 210 40% 98%;
    --primary: 217 91% 60%;
    --primary-foreground: 222.2 47.4% 11.2%;
  }
}

// Consumer: tailwind.config.ts in any app
import { hrPlatformPreset } from '@hr-platform/tokens';
export default {
  presets: [hrPlatformPreset],
  content: ['./src/**/*.{ts,tsx,html}'],
} satisfies Config;

Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

After publishing @hr-platform/tokens@2.0.0 with updated brand colors, the Angular HR Leave app looked correct but the React ContactsHub had mismatched button colors. Investigation: ContactsHub was using shadcn/ui's built-in token names (--primary) while the token package used the same name with different HSL values. The shadcn/ui installation had its own globals.css that overrode the token package's values due to CSS specificity. Fix: Aligned the token package's variable names exactly with shadcn/ui's expected names (they use the same convention). Removed the duplicate token definitions from ContactsHub's local globals.css and imported from the shared package instead. Added a CI lint rule that flagged any local CSS variable definition that shadowed a token package variable.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Token package and component library define same variablesAlign naming conventions; lint rule prevents local overrides of package tokens
Token package update breaks visual consistencyVisual regression tests run on every token update PR per consuming app
Tailwind preset can't be used by non-Tailwind appsExport tokens in multiple formats: CSS vars, JSON, SCSS variables
Token sprawl: teams add custom tokens without governanceToken addition requires design team review; max 50 semantic tokens
Purged CSS removes unused token-based classesSafelist critical token classes; ensure content paths include all component files

Validation Strategy

  • Visual Regression: Every token change triggers Playwright screenshots across all consuming apps
  • Contrast Check: Automated WCAG AA compliance for all color token pairs
  • Token Audit: Monthly review: unused tokens removed, naming consistency enforced
  • Multi-App Test: Token package CI runs integration tests against 2+ consuming apps
  • Rollback: Revert npm package version; Renovate auto-creates downgrade PR

shadcn/ui Customization — Scalable Strategy for 1M+ Users

Definition

shadcn/ui at Scale manages a component library built on Radix UI primitives + Tailwind CSS that's been copied into the project source (not installed as a dependency). At scale, the challenge is: maintaining customized components across multiple apps, syncing upstream fixes, managing component variants for different product verticals, and ensuring performance (bundle size, hydration time) as the component count grows.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

In ContactsHub, Manoj used shadcn/ui for the React 18 frontend with heavy customization: the Dialog component was extended with a confirmation variant for delete operations, the DataTable was customized for bulk CSV operations with row selection and progress indicators, and the Form components were integrated with React Hook Form for the contact creation flow. When shadcn/ui released breaking changes to the Button component (new variant API), updating was manual — Manoj had to diff his customized version against the upstream and merge carefully. He implemented a component registry that tracked which components were modified vs. vanilla, enabling targeted upstream syncing.

shadcn/ui Customization Architecture

Code Example — Component Composition Pattern

// components/ui/confirmation-dialog.tsx — composed from shadcn/ui Dialog
import {
  Dialog, DialogContent, DialogDescription,
  DialogFooter, DialogHeader, DialogTitle, DialogTrigger,
} from '@/components/ui/dialog';
import { Button } from '@/components/ui/button';
import { AlertTriangle } from 'lucide-react';

interface ConfirmationDialogProps {
  trigger: React.ReactNode;
  title: string;
  description: string;
  onConfirm: () => void;
  variant?: 'destructive' | 'warning';
  confirmText?: string;
  cancelText?: string;
}

export function ConfirmationDialog({
  trigger, title, description, onConfirm,
  variant = 'destructive',
  confirmText = 'Delete',
  cancelText = 'Cancel',
}: ConfirmationDialogProps) {
  const [open, setOpen] = useState(false);

  return (
    <Dialog open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
      <DialogTrigger asChild>{trigger}</DialogTrigger>
      <DialogContent>
        <DialogHeader>
          <DialogTitle className="flex items-center gap-2">
            <AlertTriangle className={variant === 'destructive' ? 'text-destructive' : 'text-yellow-500'} />
            {title}
          </DialogTitle>
          <DialogDescription>{description}</DialogDescription>
        </DialogHeader>
        <DialogFooter>
          <Button variant="outline" onClick={() => setOpen(false)}>{cancelText}</Button>
          <Button variant={variant} onClick={() => { onConfirm(); setOpen(false); }}>
            {confirmText}
          </Button>
        </DialogFooter>
      </DialogContent>
    </Dialog>
  );
}

// Usage: delete contact with confirmation
<ConfirmationDialog
  trigger={<Button variant="ghost" size="icon"><Trash2 /></Button>}
  title="Delete Contact?"
  description="This will permanently remove the contact and all associated data."
  onConfirm={() => deleteContact(contact.id)}
/>

Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

After upgrading to React 18.3, the shadcn/ui Command component (used for contact search) started throwing a hydration mismatch error. The issue: shadcn/ui's Command component used cmdk library which generated different HTML on server vs. client due to a role attribute difference. Since shadcn/ui components are source-copied, Manoj couldn't just bump a dependency — he had to patch the local command.tsx file. Fix: Added suppressHydrationWarning to the affected element and filed an upstream issue. The component registry tracked this as a "modified" component, so future upstream syncs would flag it for manual review. For the DataTable's bulk CSV upload, the original shadcn/ui Table component didn't support row selection state — Manoj composed a SelectableDataTable wrapper that added @tanstack/react-table row selection without modifying the base Table component.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Upstream shadcn/ui changes can't be auto-mergedComponent registry tracks vanilla vs. modified; auto-sync vanilla, manual-sync modified
Modifying base components makes upstream sync impossiblePrefer composition (wrap) over modification (edit source)
Bundle size grows with unused component codeTree-shaking; lazy load heavy components (Dialog, Sheet, Command)
Inconsistent component usage across multiple appsShared @hr-platform/ui package; consuming apps don't use shadcn CLI directly
Radix UI breaking changes require manual component updatesPin Radix versions; test all components on upgrade in isolated branch

Validation Strategy

  • Component Registry: Tracks status of each component (vanilla/modified/custom)
  • Visual Regression: Storybook + Chromatic screenshots for every component variant
  • Bundle Analysis: CI reports component contribution to bundle size; alert if total >100KB
  • Upstream Sync: Monthly check for shadcn/ui updates; auto-PR for vanilla components
  • Accessibility: axe-core tests on all components in Storybook; WCAG AA required

shadcn/ui Customization — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

Definition

shadcn/ui performance bottlenecks are the rendering, bundle size, and hydration inefficiencies that emerge when using Radix UI-based components at scale. Key bottlenecks: Radix portals creating DOM nodes outside the React tree (affecting paint performance), large bundle from importing unused component variants, CSS-in-JS overhead from class variance authority (cva), and hydration mismatches in SSR/SSG environments.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

In ContactsHub, the contact list page rendered 500+ rows with shadcn/ui components (Avatar, Badge, Button, DropdownMenu) per row. Performance profiling revealed: (1) DropdownMenu (Radix) pre-rendered 500 portal elements even when closed — 8MB DOM size. (2) The Button component's cva variants generated 2KB of class strings per render. (3) Avatar component triggered 500 image loads simultaneously. Manoj fixed these by virtualizing the list with @tanstack/react-virtual, lazy-rendering DropdownMenu content only on trigger, and implementing intersection observer-based lazy loading for avatars.

shadcn/ui Performance Bottleneck Breakdown

Code Example — Virtualized Contact List with Lazy Components

// ContactList.tsx — virtualized with lazy shadcn/ui
import { useVirtualizer } from '@tanstack/react-virtual';
import { Avatar, AvatarFallback, AvatarImage } from '@/components/ui/avatar';
import { Badge } from '@/components/ui/badge';
import { Button } from '@/components/ui/button';
import { lazy, Suspense, useRef } from 'react';

// Lazy load heavy components
const ContactActions = lazy(() => import('./ContactActions'));

export function ContactList({ contacts }: { contacts: Contact[] }) {
  const parentRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null);

  const virtualizer = useVirtualizer({
    count: contacts.length,
    getScrollElement: () => parentRef.current,
    estimateSize: () => 72,
    overscan: 10,
  });

  return (
    <div ref={parentRef} className="h-[600px] overflow-auto">
      <div style={{ height: virtualizer.getTotalSize(), position: 'relative' }}>
        {virtualizer.getVirtualItems().map((item) => {
          const contact = contacts[item.index];
          return (
            <div
              key={contact.id}
              className="absolute w-full flex items-center gap-3 px-4 py-2 border-b"
              style={{ top: item.start, height: item.size }}
            >
              <Avatar className="h-10 w-10">
                <AvatarImage
                  src={contact.avatarUrl}
                  loading="lazy" {/* Browser-native lazy loading */}
                  alt={contact.name}
                />
                <AvatarFallback>{contact.name[0]}</AvatarFallback>
              </Avatar>
              <div className="flex-1 min-w-0">
                <p className="font-medium truncate">{contact.name}</p>
                <p className="text-sm text-muted-foreground truncate">{contact.email}</p>
              </div>
              <Badge variant={contact.active ? 'default' : 'secondary'}>
                {contact.active ? 'Active' : 'Inactive'}
              </Badge>
              <Suspense fallback={<Button variant="ghost" size="icon" disabled />}>
                <ContactActions contact={contact} />
              </Suspense>
            </div>
          );
        })}
      </div>
    </div>
  );
}

Debugging Narrative — ContactsHub

The contact list page had a 3.2s First Contentful Paint and used 120MB of memory. React DevTools Profiler showed 500 DropdownMenu.Content components rendering on mount even though none were open — Radix renders portal content eagerly by default. The DOM had 15,000 nodes. Fix 1: Wrapped DropdownMenu content in a lazy-loaded component that only rendered when the trigger was clicked. Fix 2: Applied @tanstack/react-virtual to render only ~15 visible rows instead of 500. Fix 3: Avatar images used Cloudinary URLs — added loading="lazy" attribute so browsers only fetched visible images. Combined result: FCP dropped to 0.8s, DOM nodes from 15K to 800, memory from 120MB to 35MB. Also discovered that cva (class-variance-authority) re-computed class strings on every render — memoizing the variant computation with useMemo eliminated 2ms per component render.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Radix portals render eagerly for all list itemsLazy-render portal content; only mount on trigger interaction
500+ component instances kill render performanceVirtualize lists; only render visible items + overscan buffer
Avatar images load all at onceloading="lazy" attribute; IntersectionObserver for custom lazy loading
cva re-computes class strings on every renderMemoize cva output with useMemo; or pre-compute at module level
Bundle includes unused component variantsImport specific components, not barrel exports; analyze with bundle-analyzer

Validation Strategy

  • Performance Budget: FCP <1s, LCP <2s, DOM nodes <1000 for list pages
  • Memory Profiling: Chrome DevTools heap snapshot; must stay <50MB for 500-item lists
  • Bundle Analysis: Per-component bundle contribution tracked; no single component >15KB
  • Lighthouse CI: Performance score >90 on every PR for affected pages
  • Render Profiler: React DevTools render time per component; alert if any >16ms

Angular Material Theming — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

Definition

Angular Material theming bottlenecks are the CSS generation, runtime performance, and bundle size issues that emerge when customizing Angular Material's theme system. Key bottlenecks: SCSS compilation time for custom themes, large CSS output from including all component themes (~80KB+), runtime theme switching overhead, and the specificity wars between Tailwind utilities and Material's generated styles.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's CRM Dashboard

The CRM Sales Dashboard used Angular Material with a custom theme. Manoj encountered a 73KB CSS bundle just from Angular Material's theme — because the default @include mat.all-component-themes($theme) included styles for all 30+ Material components, even though the app only used 8 (Button, Card, Table, Dialog, Snackbar, Toolbar, Sidenav, Form Field). He also used Tailwind CSS alongside Material, causing specificity conflicts where Tailwind's p-4 was overridden by Material's internal padding styles. Instrumentation revealed that switching to component-level theme includes and adjusting specificity with @layer reduced CSS from 73KB to 18KB.

Angular Material Theme Bottleneck Breakdown

Code Example — Optimized Angular Material Theme

// styles/theme.scss — optimized component-level theming
@use '@angular/material' as mat;

// Define custom palette
$primary: mat.m2-define-palette(mat.$m2-indigo-palette);
$accent: mat.m2-define-palette(mat.$m2-blue-palette);
$warn: mat.m2-define-palette(mat.$m2-red-palette);

$light-theme: mat.m2-define-light-theme((
  color: (primary: $primary, accent: $accent, warn: $warn),
  typography: mat.m2-define-typography-config(),
  density: 0,
));

// Include ONLY used component themes (not all-component-themes)
@include mat.core();
@include mat.button-theme($light-theme);
@include mat.card-theme($light-theme);
@include mat.table-theme($light-theme);
@include mat.dialog-theme($light-theme);
@include mat.snack-bar-theme($light-theme);
@include mat.toolbar-theme($light-theme);
@include mat.sidenav-theme($light-theme);
@include mat.form-field-theme($light-theme);

// Dark theme override using CSS class
.dark-theme {
  $dark-theme: mat.m2-define-dark-theme((
    color: (primary: $primary, accent: $accent, warn: $warn),
  ));
  @include mat.button-color($dark-theme);
  @include mat.card-color($dark-theme);
  @include mat.table-color($dark-theme);
  @include mat.dialog-color($dark-theme);
  // ... only color overrides, not typography/density
}

// Resolve Tailwind vs Material specificity
@layer material, tailwind;
@layer material {
  // Material styles get lower specificity
}

Debugging Narrative — CRM Dashboard

The CRM Dashboard's CSS bundle was 73KB just from Angular Material. Using Chrome DevTools' Coverage tab, Manoj found that 75% of Material CSS was unused (component styles for MatChipList, MatStepper, MatSlider, etc. that the app didn't use). Fix: Replaced @include mat.all-component-themes() with individual component includes for the 8 used components. CSS dropped from 73KB to 18KB. The Tailwind conflict was subtler: mat-card had padding: 16px with high specificity from Material's styles, and Tailwind's p-4 (also 16px) was being ignored on some cards but not others. Root cause: Angular's ViewEncapsulation added _ngcontent attributes to Material's styles, boosting their specificity above Tailwind's flat utility classes. Fix: Used @layer directive to put Material styles in a lower-priority layer, and added !important only for the 3 conflicting cases (documented with comments explaining why).

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
all-component-themes includes 30+ unused component stylesUse individual component-theme includes; list only used components
Tailwind and Material CSS specificity conflicts@layer directive; Tailwind in higher layer; avoid !important except documented cases
Dark theme doubles CSS size (all themes re-included)Use -color() mixins for dark theme (color-only, skip typography/density)
SCSS compilation slow with full Material importsUse @use with specific component paths instead of entire @angular/material
ViewEncapsulation boosts Material specificity unpredictablyUse ViewEncapsulation.None for theme wrapper; or ::ng-deep for overrides (deprecated but pragmatic)

Validation Strategy

  • CSS Budget: Material theme CSS must be <25KB; total CSS <50KB
  • Coverage Check: Chrome DevTools Coverage tab; <20% unused CSS
  • Specificity Audit: No unintentional !important usage; lint rule for documented exceptions only
  • Theme Switch Test: Dark mode toggle measured at <100ms; no FOUC
  • Visual Regression: Screenshots in light + dark mode for all Material component usages

Angular Material Theming — Interviewer-Grade Deep Dive

Definition

Angular Material Theming provides a SCSS-based system for applying consistent colors, typography, and density to Material Design components in Angular applications. The deep dive covers: the Material Design 2 (M2) vs M3 theming APIs, trade-offs between pre-built themes and custom palettes, failure modes in theme configuration (color contrast, dark mode gaps), and rollback strategies for theme changes that break accessibility or visual consistency.

Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

The HR Leave Management System used Angular 18 with Angular Material and needed to match VisionQuest Solutions' corporate branding (specific indigo/blue palette). Manoj created a custom M2 theme with corporate primary and accent colors, then extended it with Tailwind CSS for layout utilities. The biggest challenge was maintaining WCAG AA contrast ratios across both light and dark modes — the corporate blue (#3F51B5) had insufficient contrast against white text in some Material components (chips, badges). He built a contrast validation pipeline that checked every color token pair against WCAG AA requirements during CI.

Angular Material Theming Deep Dive

Code Example — Custom Theme with Contrast Validation

// scripts/validate-contrast.ts — WCAG AA contrast checker
interface ColorPair {
  name: string;
  foreground: string; // hex
  background: string; // hex
  minRatio: number; // 4.5 for normal text, 3 for large text
}

function hexToRgb(hex: string): [number, number, number] {
  const r = parseInt(hex.slice(1, 3), 16);
  const g = parseInt(hex.slice(3, 5), 16);
  const b = parseInt(hex.slice(5, 7), 16);
  return [r, g, b];
}

function luminance(r: number, g: number, b: number): number {
  const [rs, gs, bs] = [r, g, b].map(c => {
    c = c / 255;
    return c <= 0.03928 ? c / 12.92 : Math.pow((c + 0.055) / 1.055, 2.4);
  });
  return 0.2126 * rs + 0.7152 * gs + 0.0722 * bs;
}

function contrastRatio(hex1: string, hex2: string): number {
  const l1 = luminance(...hexToRgb(hex1));
  const l2 = luminance(...hexToRgb(hex2));
  const lighter = Math.max(l1, l2);
  const darker = Math.min(l1, l2);
  return (lighter + 0.05) / (darker + 0.05);
}

// Token pairs to validate
const pairs: ColorPair[] = [
  { name: 'primary-on-white', foreground: '#3F51B5', background: '#FFFFFF', minRatio: 4.5 },
  { name: 'primary-on-dark', foreground: '#7986CB', background: '#121212', minRatio: 4.5 },
  { name: 'accent-on-white', foreground: '#2196F3', background: '#FFFFFF', minRatio: 4.5 },
  { name: 'destructive-on-white', foreground: '#F44336', background: '#FFFFFF', minRatio: 4.5 },
];

let failed = false;
for (const pair of pairs) {
  const ratio = contrastRatio(pair.foreground, pair.background);
  const status = ratio >= pair.minRatio ? 'PASS' : 'FAIL';
  console.log(`${status}: ${pair.name} — ratio ${ratio.toFixed(2)} (min: ${pair.minRatio})`);
  if (status === 'FAIL') failed = true;
}

if (failed) process.exit(1);

Debugging Narrative — HR Leave System

After applying the corporate blue palette, the mat-chip component for leave type badges (Annual, Sick, Casual) had white text on a light blue background — contrast ratio was only 2.8:1 (WCAG AA requires 4.5:1). The Material theme system auto-generated the chip text color from the palette's contrast property, but the corporate palette didn't define contrast colors for the 200-400 shades. Fix: (1) Added explicit contrast values to the custom palette for all shades 50-400 (using dark text instead of white). (2) Created the contrast validation script above and added it to CI. (3) For the dark mode, the 700 shade of the corporate blue was used as the chip background (better contrast with white text). The Tailwind integration issue was more complex: mat-toolbar used padding: 0 16px from Material, but the layout needed px-6 (24px) from Tailwind. Because of ViewEncapsulation, Tailwind's class was ignored. Fix: Added ::ng-deep .mat-toolbar { @apply px-6; } with a comment explaining the override.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Custom palette missing contrast colors for light shadesDefine explicit contrast values for all 14 shades; CI contrast check
M2 to M3 migration breaks existing themePin Angular Material version; plan migration as dedicated sprint
ViewEncapsulation blocks Tailwind overridesUse ::ng-deep sparingly with comments; or set ViewEncapsulation.None for layout components
Dark mode SCSS doubles the CSS bundleUse color-only mixins for dark theme; CSS custom properties for dynamic switching
Theme changes not visible until full rebuildAngular CLI hot reload supports SCSS changes; use ng serve for dev feedback

Validation Strategy

  • Contrast CI Gate: All color token pairs must meet WCAG AA (4.5:1 for normal text)
  • Visual Regression: Playwright screenshots of all themed components in both modes
  • CSS Size Budget: Material theme CSS <25KB after component-level includes
  • Accessibility Audit: axe-core scan on every themed page; zero critical violations
  • Rollback: Theme change = CSS file change → revert + rebuild takes <3 min
